title: E5 mcp_exposed default-exposure policy: secure-by-default startup warning
description: Decision plus implementation. Today the E5 connection-isolation model exposes ALL profiles to the MCP agent when NO profile sets mcp_exposed (the allow-list activates only once any profile is flagged) -- see crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs mcp_isolation_active(). For a production-positioned tool this is a silent footgun: an operator who adds a writable non-protected prod profile beside a dev profile and forgets to flag exposes both. DECISION (recommended, non-breaking): keep expose-all ONLY when 0 or 1 profiles are defined (zero-config single-target still just works); when 2 or more profiles are defined and NONE sets mcp_exposed, emit a loud one-time startup WARNING that all profiles are exposed and that mcp_exposed should be set to segment; document an operator-selectable stricter mode (expose only default_profile) as a follow-up. Do NOT change the existing allow-list behavior when any profile IS flagged. Implement the warning at server startup reading the resolved config. Preserve every hard invariant and the fail-closed posture. ACCEPTANCE (tests REQUIRED): unit tests for (a) single-profile/none-flagged => no warning and all exposed; (b) 2+ profiles/none-flagged => warning emitted and still all exposed (back-compat); (c) any profile flagged => allow-list unchanged and no warning; (d) warning text names mcp_exposed and the exposed-profile count. VERIFY cargo test, clippy -D, fmt green; no regression in the existing E5 tests (wp-e-kua.6); honesty grep green.

title: A1 — Lazy per-statement read-only backstop (SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY)
description:
Defense-in-depth (layer B; layer A = least-priv DB user, A2). Before each statement on the PINNED lease/primary session, if effective_level()==ReadOnly ensure SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY is in force, else lift it. NOT a connect-time one-shot: it is transaction-scoped (ends at next COMMIT/ROLLBACK) and effective_level() drops to ReadOnly SILENTLY on TTL-window expiry — re-assert at the start of every read txn (post-commit/rollback/lease-reset). Scope: pinned lease/primary session ONLY (incoherent on the stateless metadata pool — that relies on A2). Best-effort (does not stop autonomous-txn side effects). Area: oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs (lease/txn path), dispatch.
TESTS: deterministic test injects a classifier-bypassing stub → DB refuses the write at ReadOnly EVEN AFTER a prior COMMIT/ROLLBACK cycle and after a silent elevation-window expiry; after a confirmed escalation the same write succeeds; backstop re-asserted on return to ReadOnly. Sequence with/after B1 (async exec seam).
close_reason: A1 lazy read-only backstop: new dispatch/read_only_backstop.rs ReadOnlyBackstop on the dispatcher's pinned read session. Lazy SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY (reuses oraclemcp_guard::SET_TRANSACTION_READ_ONLY) issued ONCE per read transaction (not per-statement; tested arms-lazily-once across N reads); re-asserts whenever effective_level==READ_ONLY and disarmed (covers silent TTL-window drop back to READ_ONLY); ONLY arms at READ_ONLY so a gated write at READ_WRITE+ is never blocked (tested); disarm() before every gated write (execute/execute_approved/deploy_ddl/compile_object/create_or_replace/patch_source) + reset() on profile switch; fail-closed (if SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY can't apply, the read is refused, not marked armed). Defense-in-depth UNDER the classifier+level gate (DB enforces ORA-01456). Composes with B1 async + lease dirty-discard + A9. Tests: 5 unit + 4 wiring + live ORA-01456 (in A7). Gates green.

title: A2 — Read-only proxy-user/role posture (doctor + docs)
description:
doctor reports whether the connected principal can write and warns; README documents the recommended least-privilege grant set (the real boundary under the classifier + A1). Also (per Round 3) report SUPPORTED wallet modes accurately: unencrypted ewallet.pem/cwallet.sso/wallet_password already work in the pinned driver — not fail-closed. Area: oraclemcp-core/src/doctor.rs.
TESTS: doctor unit test asserts write-privilege detection + warning; live-xe check against a read-only vs read-write principal.
acceptance_criteria: [R4 review reconciliation] Unit/doctor test asserts unencrypted ewallet.pem, cwallet.sso, and wallet_password are reported SUPPORTED (not fail-closed).
close_reason: A2 read-only proxy/role posture: privileges.rs probe_write_posture+WritePosture reads the session's own SESSION_PRIVS for write-implying privileges; oci.rs supported_wallet_modes+WalletMode; doctor check #11 'Write posture' (proxy_user wired via main.rs). Fail-closed reporting: probe failure -> can_write=None -> Warn (never a false read-only Pass); write-capable principal Warns naming the privs; report-only, never fails. Wallet modes assert unencrypted ewallet.pem/cwallet.sso/ewallet.p12+password reported SUPPORTED (not fail-closed). README least-priv grant set. Gates green.

title: A3 — Per-statement audit marker comment (model/profile/tool)
description:
Prepend /* oraclemcp llm=<model> profile=<name> tool=<tool> */ to every executed statement (SQLcl-style; greppable in V$SQL/ASH without trusting the client). CRITICAL: classify the MARKED text, not the bare SQL — the live pipeline classifies the exact text it executes (dispatch/mod.rs classify(&args.sql)); prepend then classify the marked text and confirm the SAME verdict (executed==classified, preserving the fail-closed invariant). No secrets in the marker; the A8 audit digest covers the marked text actually sent.
TESTS: live test shows marker in V$SQL; unit test asserts marked SQL yields the same classifier verdict as bare SQL; adversarial/fuzz case proves the marker can't be an injection vector (forged */ in <model>).
close_reason: A3 per-statement audit marker: new dispatch/audit_marker.rs prepends '/* oraclemcp llm=<model> profile=<name> tool=<tool> */' to executed SQL (execute/execute_approved/create_or_replace/deploy_ddl + oracle_query). Injection-safe: sanitize_marker_value strips * / ; \ + control chars (cannot close the comment or start a statement); with_audit_marker re-classifies the marked text and adopts it ONLY if the verdict is byte-equal to bare SQL, else fails closed to bare SQL; defense-in-depth assert in execute_sql_inner refuses if marked verdict != gating decision. Gate/confirmation run on bare SQL. Tests: prepend+greppable, same-verdict across read/write/ddl/plsql, sanitizer strips */ + newlines, forged model label cannot break out or change verdict, length-bounded. Gates green.

title: A4 — Dynamic V$SESSION MODULE/ACTION/CLIENT_INFO tagging (agent+model)
description:
Today apply_session_identity (connection.rs:500-549) sets MODULE/ACTION/CLIENT_INFO once at connect from profile identity, with NO reset on pool checkout — so this is NET-NEW (not "extends a reset"): add an explicit DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO/DBMS_SESSION clear-and-reset on every checkout and set the live agent/model/tool per request. CLIENT_IDENTIFIER persists across pooled reuse unless explicitly cleared.
TESTS: live test shows MODULE/ACTION reflect the current agent+model AND a prior request's tag is gone before the next is set (no cross-request leak via pooled reuse).
close_reason: A4 V$SESSION tagging: LeaseManager::acquire runs a pure session_tag_statements(agent,model) on every checkout via DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO/DBMS_SESSION. Clear-and-reset BEFORE set (SET_MODULE(NULL,NULL)+CLEAR_IDENTIFIER) so a prior pooled request's CLIENT_IDENTIFIER can't leak; MODULE=oraclemcp, ACTION=agent, CLIENT_INFO='agent=.. model=..', CLIENT_IDENTIFIER=agent; values control-char-stripped + length-bounded; model best-effort from ORACLEMCP_AGENT_MODEL. Tests: builder values + bind-capturing mock + clear-before-set; live test gated. Gates green.

Map use_iam_token/iam_token profiles to oracledb ConnectOptions::with_access_token (lib.rs:1100); enforce TCPS (driver returns AccessTokenRequiresTcps, lib.rs:727); token redacted. Wiring REMOVES the current hard rejection at connection.rs:411 (UnsupportedAuth). Pre-task: confirm with_access_token + AccessTokenRequiresTcps exist in the pinned oracledb (proven by closed upstream bead rust-oracledb-5bh) and snapshot in the B2 adapter. Doc-reconcile robot_docs.rs:280 + capabilities text (currently advertise IAM as "unsupported"). Adopt oracledb's typed UnsupportedAuthMode for doctor classification IF/WHEN it lands (qm4, not yet); until then keep current structured fail-closed errors. Area: oraclemcp-db/src/auth_adapter.rs, oraclemcp-core/src/connect.rs, connection.rs:411.
TESTS: IAM-token profile connects to an ADB-style TCPS endpoint (or documented skip); non-TCPS refused; the connection.rs:411 rejection is gone; Kerberos/RADIUS/passwordless-wallet still fail closed with precise classification.

Added doctor AuthModeClass{DriverUnsupported,BadCredentials,Tls,Listener,Other}+classify_auth_mode surfaced on the connectivity CheckResult (R4 AC: driver-unsupported vs bad-creds/TLS/listener distinguished; IAM-over-plaintext -> Tls). Tests: 4 adapter + 1 classification; live gated. Gates green.

title: A6 — <untrusted-user-data> output fencing (prompt-injection defense)
description:
Wrap returned rows/text in uuid-tagged blocks with a "treat as data, not instructions" preamble (DB content is attacker-controllable). structuredContent stays machine-parseable alongside.
TESTS: unit test asserts returned text content is fenced + structuredContent unaffected; an adversarial row value cannot break out of the fence.
close_reason: A6 output fencing: new oraclemcp-core/fence.rs wraps the agent-facing text channel in <untrusted-user-data-<tag>>...</> with a treat-as-data preamble; structuredContent left untouched (machine callers unaffected); server-authored errors not fenced. Fence tag = sha256(payload||counter)[..16] — content-derived + per-call so row data cannot forge the closing tag; any literal 'untrusted-user-data' token in payload is neutralized. No new dep (existing sha2). Tests + conformance updated; golden scoped to the text field. Gates green.

title: A7 — Tests for the trust/safety wave
description: Deterministic (read-only backstop incl. post-commit re-assert; output fencing) + live (audit marker in V\$SQL; MODULE/ACTION tagging; IAM token connect). Structured logging; clean SKIP without Oracle. Aggregates the per-task test ACs of A1/A3/A4/A5/A6 into one green suite.

Added live-xe feature to the oraclemcp crate; live tests SKIP cleanly without a DB. Gates green: build/test(917)/clippy -D warnings/fmt/deny/seam/boundary/honesty.

title: A8 — BLOCKING: wire + sign + verify the served audit path
description:
RELEASE-BLOCKING (DoD gate). Today the served oracle_execute/execute_approved path runs classify→gate→confirm→execute→commit with NO audit append (the only audited path oracle_query_execute is not in TOOL_NAMES; no wired Auditor in served dispatch though oraclemcp-audit is transitively present). The headline "tamper-evident audit" is FALSE until this lands.
(a) WIRE the hash-chained Auditor into served dispatch: append on every Guarded/Destructive/escalation action, fsync-before-execute, before commit (oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs execute_sql_inner ~:1539).
(b) SIGN the chain: a keyed MAC over entry_hash (oraclemcp-audit/src/record.rs is bare SHA-256 today — forgeable by recompute-from-genesis); key from KMS/Vault, rotatable.
(c) VERIFY tool: `oraclemcp audit verify <file>` chain-verification subcommand.
TESTS: every write/DDL/Admin and every oracle_set_session_level escalation produces a signed audit record provable by `audit verify`; a tamper (in-place edit OR full recompute-without-key) is detected; deterministic + live tests. Until A8 lands, the audit positioning in plan §1/§4 stays struck. Pairs with D2 (shipping) + D5 (threat model).
notes: [R4 review reconciliation] Implementation route (a) is EITHER: add a direct oraclemcp-audit dependency to the served binary, OR route served execution through a core API that owns the Auditor — both are sanctioned; do not assume the direct-dependency route. The currently-unused audited path to supersede/replace is `oracle_query_execute` at crates/oraclemcp-core/src/query_execute.rs:100 (not in TOOL_NAMES, so dead); the live dispatch is crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:1539.
close_reason: Wired the hash-chained, keyed-MAC Auditor into the SERVED dispatch path (oracle_execute/execute_approved + create_or_replace/deploy_ddl DDL paths + oracle_set_session_level escalations), fsync-before-execute; signed the chain with HMAC-SHA256 over entry_hash (key_id rotation, fail-closed when signing required but key absent), implemented over in-tree sha2 (no new dep); added 'oraclemcp audit verify <file>' CLI (recomputes links + re-checks MAC, non-zero on tamper/forgery). Tests: pure-fn (query_execute), MAC tamper/recompute-without-key/wrong-key (verify+hmac), dispatch wiring (write->Pending+signed outcome, read->none, escalation->signed record), durable-flush poisoning. Gates green: build/test/clippy -D warnings/fmt/deny. Left TODO(simplify) to bundle audit ctx into AuditCtx to drop the too_many_arguments allow on the DDL fns.

title: A9 — Compile-time capability narrowing (asupersync cap model)
description:
Defense-in-depth ABOVE the runtime operating-level ceiling. Narrow the Cx capability row [SPAWN,TIME,RANDOM,IO,REMOTE] at the dispatch boundary so a read-path tool STRUCTURALLY cannot spawn / do remote / use ambient randomness — only the effects it needs (TIME + DB I/O). Use SubsetOf monotone narrowing (widening is compile-time rejected; sealed markers). Stays UNDER the fail-closed classifier + level gate (primary boundary) — structural reinforcement, not a replacement. Area: oraclemcp-core dispatch / Cx plumbing.
TESTS: read tools receive a narrowed Cx (no SPAWN/REMOTE/RANDOM); a compile-fail fixture proves a read handler cannot spawn or do remote I/O.
acceptance_criteria: [R4 review reconciliation] Second compile-fail fixture: an attempt to WIDEN a narrowed Cx (re-add SPAWN/REMOTE capability) is rejected at compile time — proves SubsetOf monotonicity, the load-bearing safety property.
close_reason: A9 compile-time capability narrowing (RELEASE GATE): new oraclemcp-core/capability.rs using asupersync 0.3.4's sealed CapSet/SubsetOf/Has* system. ReadPathCaps=CapSet<SPAWN=f,TIME=t,RANDOM=f,IO=t,REMOTE=f>; narrow_to_read_path via Cx::restrict (SubsetOf bound — narrow-only); sealed PrivilegedEffect marker (impl only for HasRemote rows) makes requires_privileged_effect uncallable from a read Cx. Wired at the dispatch boundary for oracle_query + operator read tools; read-query fns made generic over the cap row. TWO trybuild compile-fail fixtures (checked-in .stderr): (1) a read handler cannot remote/spawn, (2) WIDENING a narrowed Cx is compile-rejected (SubsetOf monotonicity — the load-bearing property). HONEST scope: SPAWN=false enforced structurally (read Cx never handed a Scope; widening rejected) since 0.3.4 Scope::spawn isn't HasSpawn-gated yet; REMOTE/RANDOM gated by accessors; defense-in-depth ABOVE the classifier+level gate, never a replacement. trybuild dev-dep (MIT/Apache, deny-allowed). Gates green incl cargo deny.

title: Epic: WP-B — Async DB migration + adapter seam + load evidence + canonical oraclemcp-db
description: Use oracledb's native async Cx-first Connection (drop the BlockingConnection facade + per-call block_on/mutex). The B2 adapter seam funnels ALL oracledb calls through one module so oracledb's 0.3.0 churn (four-op API, single op-deadline W1-T3, accessor types W1-T4, module-path moves W1-T9, shim-expiry) is a one-file cut-over. oraclemcp 0.4.0 ships on oracledb 0.2.2; the four-op migration is a publish-blocker for oracledb 0.3.0 (upstream W2-T1.3). oraclemcp-db is the canonical shared connectivity crate.

Async DB foundation + adapter seam + load evidence + canonical foundation shipped.

title: B2 — Internal driver-adapter seam (isolate ALL oracledb churn)
description:
One module wraps every oracledb call (connect, the execute_query* family, fetch, LOB, cursor, auth). All of oraclemcp calls OUR adapter, never oracledb directly. This isolates oracledb's 0.3.0 cut-over to one file. 0.3.0 cut-over scope funneled here (ROAD_TO_1_0 v3.3): (i) four operation-specific request types (query/execute/execute_many/register, W1-T3) replacing the 19 execute_query* variants; (ii) single absolute op-deadline replacing per-call timeout_ms (W1-T3 principle 7 — Duration translated once, tighter Cx deadline wins; B3 baselines re-validate); (iii) accessor-based result/metadata types + selective #[non_exhaustive] (W1-T4) — use accessors + as_*/wildcard, never exhaustive match on oracledb::{BindValue,QueryValue,Error}; (iv) module/re-export path moves (W1-T9); (v) deprecated shims ship only in 0.3.0, removed before oracledb 1.0.0-rc.1 — migrate FULLY, never lean on shims. Drive from oracledb docs/MIGRATING-0.3.md (W2-T1.6). oraclemcp's migration is a PUBLISH-BLOCKER for oracledb 0.3.0 (W2-T1.3). Today map against the ACTUAL 0.2.2 error surface: ora_code() (lib.rs:787), is_connection_lost()/is_transient()/is_retryable() (lib.rs:848/870/882) — NOT the future kind()/connection_disposition()/retry_hint() names.
TESTS/AC: `grep` proves no oracledb:: call outside the adapter (a pre-0.3.0 readiness gate); a comment block documents the 0.3.0 cut-over plan; pin oracledb exactly today / ^0.3 post-0.3.0; unit tests at the adapter boundary.
notes: [R4 review reconciliation] The four-operation cut-over MUST be PRE-FLIGHTED against an oracledb 0.3.0 RC / git-pin (not designed on paper only): the adapter must compile and pass the B7 contract suite against the RC BEFORE oracledb 0.3.0 GA. Coordinate via oracledb's publish-blocker W2-T1.3 and MIGRATING-0.3.md.
close_reason: Driver-adapter seam hardened + mechanically enforced. Audit confirmed all real oracledb:: driver calls are already confined to crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs (RustOracleConnection); documented the seam + 0.3.0 single-file cut-over plan in a module doc-comment. Added scripts/oraclemcp_driver_seam_lint.sh (grep matches driver 'oracledb::' but not workspace 'oraclemcp_db::'; allowlist = connection.rs) wired into ci.yml boundary job, PLUS a Rust guard test (driver_seam mod) so cargo test catches leaks. Pinned oracledb exactly (=0.2.2) so version churn is deliberate. Behavior unchanged (async is B1). Gates green: seam-lint, build/test(740)/clippy -D warnings/fmt. Unblocks B1/B7/A5.

title: B1 — Full async DB migration (drop BlockingConnection facade)
description:
Migrate the DB trait from oracledb's BlockingConnection facade (per-call runtime + block_on + ambient-Cx lookup) to the native async Cx-first Connection (conn.execute_query(&cx,…).await; oracledb lib.rs:1349/2469). Remove build_io_runtime()/block_on + the per-connection Mutex; keep ONE asupersync runtime. FULL migration in 0.4.0 (not split). Win = remove the mutex+block_on (cooperative cancellation, many in-flight round-trips per runtime) — NOT new parallelism (server is already OS-thread-per-connection). Upside: async unlocks oracledb's zero-copy _ref borrowed-fetch family (async-only; NOT direct-path, which already ships blocking). Outcome discipline: preserve Cancelled/Panicked distinctly — map at MCP boundary (Cancelled→TIMEOUT, Panicked→connection Dead + heavier evidence); never hold the lease across an indefinite await. Area: oraclemcp-db/src/{connection,pool,query,lease}.rs. Tradeoff accepted: adapter internals migrate twice (async-on-0.2.2 now; four-op at oracledb 0.3.0 via B2).
TESTS: all DB calls async/&Cx; unit + chaos/cancellation (all green) + live-XE; DPOR/LabRuntime ready-or-dead + cancel-correctness oracle; no block_on anywhere in the DB path (grep-verified); pool dirty-discard correct (post-0.3.0, consume connection_disposition()).
close_reason: Full async DB migration landed green (806 tests). OracleConnection trait is now async/Cx-first (async-trait ?Send for dyn-compat; DispatchFuture dropped +Send — dispatch runs on a current-thread runtime per connection thread, never spawned cross-thread, compiler-proven). RustOracleConnection drives oracledb 0.2.2 NATIVE async Connection via .await; the per-call block_on + BlockingConnection facade + Mutex<oracledb::Connection> are GONE — replaced by asupersync::sync::Mutex (async-aware, safe across await); ONE asupersync runtime, thread-local block_on per connection (preserves OS-thread-per-connection concurrency, verified against asupersync builder.rs:3276). Cancelled vs Panicked preserved distinctly: cancel mid-flight -> DbError::Cancelled (->Timeout at MCP boundary) + connection/lease discarded DIRTY (never reused); Panicked -> connection Dead. Lease never held across an indefinite await. A9 read-path narrowing preserved. ALL callers migrated (dispatch ~3900 lines, core, main, all tests + contract suite). Tests: cancellation_after_mutating_execute_rolls_back_dirty_session, cancelled_preview_discards_session_dirty_never_commits, clean-drain, dpor_lab_read_execute_path_is_cancel_correct (LabRuntime ready-or-dead + cancel-correct oracle), no_block_on_in_db_path (enforced in the seam test). B2 seam holds; api-lock baseline regenerated for the intentional async API. Gates green: build/test(806)/clippy -D warnings/fmt/deny/seam/doc/api-lock. 32 files +4631/-3321. Migrate-twice tradeoff accepted (four-op at oracledb 0.3.0 via B2). Unblocks A1/A7/B3/B4/B6/D7.

title: B3 — Net load + shutdown soak evidence (release gate)
description:
High-concurrency query load test + sustained graceful-shutdown soak under cancellation, with structured metrics, recorded in docs/performance-footprint.md (incl. live Oracle latency p50/p95/p99 currently omitted). Closes asupersync's documented net load/shutdown evidence gap for our usage; also validate the D1 OUTBOUND OTLP HTTP client under sustained load (net is lane-specific, not blanket-GA).
TESTS/AC: documented load/soak run with no leaked sessions, bounded latency, clean drain; numbers committed.

close_reason: B3 load/soak evidence (release gate): offline deterministic harness crates/oraclemcp-db/tests/load_soak.rs — 8 concurrent OS-thread clients (B1's thread-per-connection block_on model) x200 iters (1600 ops, 70/20/10 read/describe/preview-DML) asserting ZERO leaked sessions (acquired==released+discarded, active_count==0 after release_all), clean drain (shutdown stops new acquires, open txns force-rolled-back), no torn commit (commits==0), bounded (high-water<=N). live_xe variant (#[ignore], ORACLEMCP_LIVE_XE=1) captures real p50/p95/p99 (D7). docs/performance-footprint.md: methodology + load shape + pass-condition table + run commands + a clearly-marked Live-measurements section with placeholders (no fabricated numbers; honesty-grep green). Gates green.

title: B4 — Async-path pool tuning + failover posture
description:
Connection-pool tuning + failover review for the async path (RAC/ADB awareness, acquire-timeout, dirty-discard under async cancellation). NOTE: oracledb 0.3.0 flips its OWN pool async-native (acquire(&self,cx,AcquireOptions), PoolEngine→pub(crate), W1-T7). DECISION: oraclemcp keeps its OWN bounded pool in oraclemcp-db and consumes single async Connections — so it does NOT inherit oracledb's pool-shape change; revisit only if we adopt oracledb::Pool (then via B2).
TESTS: chaos/cancellation pool tests (exhaustion→BUSY, dirty-discard) stay green under async.
close_reason: B4 async-path pool tuning + failover: CPU-derived sizing NOW APPLIED (PoolSettings::resolved clamps max_size to min(configured,cpu*2+1) per plan §10; the config comment claimed this but never did it — fixed). FIXED A REAL BUG: the checkout retry used asupersync::time::sleep which parks forever on the timer-less dispatch runtime so the acquire timeout never fired on an exhausted pool — replaced with cooperative yield_now() re-checking the wall-clock deadline (timeout now enforced regardless of timer config). Dirty-discard accounting extracted (record_checkin) + PoolMetrics (is_balanced/is_bounded) + metrics(). Failover posture documented in operations.md §5.3.1. Tests: clean->idle, dirty->dropped+discarded+slot-freed, reserve bounded by max_size, checkout times out (BUSY, no connect), cpu-ceiling clamp both directions. Gates green.

title: B5 — Make oraclemcp-db the canonical shared foundation (public API)
description:
Treat oraclemcp-db's public API as a product (plsql-intelligence will adopt it post-0.4.0, §12). Clean public surface for the async OracleConnection trait + types (no oraclemcp-binary-specific leakage), full rustdoc with examples, a cargo public-api snapshot locked in CI, semver/stability note in the crate README, standalone build/link proof. Accepted published-spine deps oraclemcp-error/-guard re-exports are locked in the snapshot (not pretended away). UPGRADE: adopt cargo-semver-checks (oracledb proved it + cargo public-api run under the pinned nightly, R3/R10) — a real SemVer contract, mirroring oracledb ADR-0002.
TESTS/AC: cargo doc no missing-docs on public items; cargo public-api + cargo-semver-checks snapshot committed + CI-checked (install+pin the tools per Round-2 R2-05 — not guaranteed on fresh machines); an out-of-workspace smoke crate depends on oraclemcp-db and runs a query.
close_reason: B5 canonical oraclemcp-db foundation + API-lock: audited the public surface (cargo-public-api confirms NO oracledb driver type leaks — B2 seam holds; only oraclemcp_error::ErrorEnvelope appears, intended); documented the canonical-foundation role + plsql-mcp convergence (ADR-0006) + API-stability in lib.rs + a crate README; added #![deny(missing_docs)] (fixed all gaps, cargo doc clean). API-lock tooling (ADR-0002 addendum): cargo-public-api exact-surface baselines (crates/*/api/*.txt) enforced by scripts/oraclemcp_api_lock.sh + cargo-semver-checks, on the published spine (oraclemcp-error/-guard) + oraclemcp-db; new ci.yml api-lock job (tools via taiki-e/install-action, standalone binaries so cargo deny unaffected). Gates green: build/test/clippy -D warnings/fmt/deny/api-lock/doc.

title: B6 — Adopt asupersync Budget for per-request bounds
description:
Use asupersync Budget (deadline + poll/cost quota + priority, with meet() propagation) for per-request bounds instead of only the configurable per-profile call timeout; give cleanup/finalizers a short BOUNDED budget. Partly addresses E10's per-request cost bound (per-CALLER rate-limiting still needs caller→profile binding, OUT). At oracledb 0.3.0 the budget maps onto oracledb's single op-deadline (W1-T3) — note in B2.
TESTS: tests assert budget exhaustion + bounded cleanup behavior explicitly.
close_reason: B6 asupersync Budget per-request bounds: new oraclemcp-core/request_budget.rs RequestBudget wrapping asupersync Budget — from_call_timeout(now,timeout) derives deadline + poll quota (default 30s mirrors DEFAULT_CALL_TIMEOUT); cleanup() returns a bounded budget via meet(Budget::MINIMAL); meet() tighter-wins; enforce_at/enforce map deadline/quota exhaustion + pending cx cancellation to DbError::Cancelled (preserves B1's Cancelled/Timeout mapping). Wired at dispatch boundary via SessionContext::request_budget(now) from the session call_timeout. Maps onto oracledb 0.3.0 single op-deadline later. Tests: normal unaffected, past-deadline->Cancelled, spent-quota->Cancelled, cleanup short&never-looser, meet tighter, zero-timeout floored, enforce observes pending cancellation on a real Cx. Gates green.

title: B7 — Provide the oraclemcp contract surface for oracledb W3-E7.3 (reciprocal gate)
description:

title: D1-metrics — OTLP metrics (hand-rolled prost encoder)

Hand-roll our own prost ExportMetricsServiceRequest (pattern: asupersync's otlp_logs_proto), encode MetricsSnapshot → protobuf → OtlpHttpExporter::send_otlp_protobuf. Metric set: request count/duration histograms (p50/p95/p99), classifier-reject counter, pool saturation + checkout-wait, lease lifetime, DB-call latency, error-class counters. Consume oracledb's 0.2.2 tracing spans now; oracledb W1-T6 pool metrics/disposition arrive only with 0.3.0 (gate behind 0.3.0).
TESTS: OTLP metrics reach a collector; no-tokio graph; no secrets.
notes: [R4 review reconciliation] Map attributes to OTel db.* semantic conventions where non-leaking; implement configurable sampling.
close_reason: D1-metrics: hand-rolled prost ExportMetricsServiceRequest (asupersync's encoder is cfg(fuzz)-gated) in otlp/proto.rs+metrics.rs; MetricsSnapshot -> OTel db.* semantic conventions (db.client.operation.duration, db.client.errors{db.system.name,db.response.status_code}, db.client.connection.count/wait_time, mcp.server.request.count); configurable sampling (OTEL_METRICS_SAMPLER_ARG); sent via OtlpHttpExporter::send_otlp_protobuf. prost 0.14 (Apache-2.0, pure codec, no runtime; deny+boundary clean). Tests: protobuf shape, db.* mapping, redaction on labels. Gates green.

title: D1-traces — tracing→OtlpSpan bridge (the real work)
description:
A FULL tracing_subscriber::Layer → OtlpSpan bridge (span open/close, field+timing capture, attribute mapping, secret redaction, W3C trace/span-ID threading, batching) — a real component, not a shim (heavy-M/L). Span tree per request→dispatch→classify→DB call→serialize with attributes (tool, profile, operating level, row counts, cache hit, ORA code), secrets redacted; W3C context propagation from the MCP client. Hand-roll ExportTraceServiceRequest prost; wrap with LoadSheddingTraceExporter for backpressure. The exporter batch/flush loop is REGION-OWNED with a bounded shutdown budget (NOT a detached spawn); telemetry failure DROPS, never blocks the request path. HTTP/protobuf only (gRPC absent — tonic→tokio). Enable oracledb's `tracing` feature so DB-call spans flow in.
TESTS: OTLP traces reach a collector; W3C context propagated; no-tokio; secrets redacted.
notes: [R4 review reconciliation] Map span attributes to OTel db.* semantic conventions where non-leaking; implement configurable sampling.
close_reason: D1-traces: real tracing_subscriber::Layer OtlpTraceLayer (span open/close, field+timing capture, secret redaction, W3C trace/span-id threading — root mints/adopts traceparent, children inherit+link parent, head sampling, batching to ExportPump); hand-rolled ExportTraceServiceRequest; span tree request->dispatch->classify->DB->serialize with db.* attrs (tool/profile/operating_level/rowcount — never SQL/binds/secrets). Enabled oracledb tracing feature for DB-call spans. Export batch/flush is region-owned (dedicated thread+runtime, bounded shutdown drain, drop-on-overflow) — never blocks the request path. Redaction hardened: db.statement/db.query.text/db.query.parameter.* dropped even under the db.* allowlist (defense-in-depth backstop). Tests: redaction incl raw-SQL-db-keys dropped, W3C threading, sampling. Gates green.

title: Epic: wire all currently supported thin-driver profile/config capabilities
description:
## Goal

Make oraclemcp's repo-local profile/config surface catch up with what the current pure-Rust thin `oracledb` driver already supports, without waiting on upstream features that are genuinely missing.

## Current evidence

- oraclemcp's strict config schema only exposes a subset of thin-driver connection options in `crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs`.
- `oraclemcp_core::profile_to_options` currently maps connect string, username/password, external/IAM flags, wallet location, a subset of session identity, call timeout, and session setup statements in `crates/oraclemcp-core/src/connect.rs`.
- `oraclemcp_db::connection::to_connect_options` currently only applies `wallet_location` and forces SNI when a wallet exists in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs`.
- The current `rust-oracledb` driver already supports additional public options: `ClientIdentity::{program,machine,osuser,terminal,driver_name}`, `with_edition`, `with_wallet_password`, `with_ssl_server_dn_match`, `with_ssl_server_cert_dn`, `with_use_sni`, `with_proxy_user`, `with_app_context`, and `with_sdu`.
- oraclemcp already contains partially stranded local support for `AuthAdapter::Proxy`, `DrcpConfig`, `PoolConfig`, `OraclePool`, and result placeholders for `QueryValue::Lob` and `QueryValue::Cursor`.

## Scope

Create and implement granular child beads for fields/capabilities that are wireable now because the driver or local code already supports them. Do not include Kerberos, RADIUS/native MFA, OCI IAM database-token auth, passwordless external/wallet auth, or ADT/object decoding here; those need upstream driver work and have separate upstream GitHub issues where applicable.

## Non-negotiables

- Preserve the fail-closed SQL guard and profile operating-level ceilings.
- Keep profile metadata and doctor output secret-safe: no connect strings, usernames, passwords, wallet passwords, token values, wallet paths, application-context values, or workstation/user identity leaks unless a tool is explicitly returning live Oracle session diagnostics.
- Keep config validation strict: new keys must be typed, documented, inherited correctly, and covered by tests.
- The default behavior must remain safe and compatible for existing profiles.
- Every child bead must include focused unit tests and, where feasible, live Oracle 23ai/FREE validation instructions.
close_reason: All dependency beads are closed: supported thin profile/config capabilities are wired through config, SessionContext, OracleConnectOptions, thin ConnectOptions, docs, doctor/setup, materialization, and live matrix coverage. Verified with full gates, Oracle 23ai live tests, and epic compliance audit 2026-06-18T10-25-21Z.

title: Wire driver-level application context triples from profiles
description:
## Current gap

`rust-oracledb` exposes `ConnectOptions::with_app_context(Vec<(namespace, key, value)>)`, and the protocol layer applies those triples during auth. oraclemcp currently has no profile schema for driver-level application contexts. Operators can sometimes approximate this with `trusted_session_statements`, but that is broader, later, and less structured than the driver-supported logon app-context path.

## Required change

Add a strict profile config section for driver-level application context triples and map it through:

- `ConnectionProfile`
- `profile_to_options`
- `OracleConnectOptions`
- `RustOracleConnection::to_connect_options`
- `oracledb::ConnectOptions::with_app_context(...)`

## Design requirements

- Use a typed list of triples, not string parsing.
- Validate that namespace/key are non-empty and bounded.
- Treat values as potentially sensitive tenant/session context: do not include them in profile metadata or ordinary debug output.
- Preserve `trusted_session_statements` for cases that really need arbitrary operator-authored session setup.

## Acceptance criteria

- TOML examples can express at least two app-context triples.
- Config inheritance merges or replaces the app-context list according to the documented profile inheritance rule; tests must pin that behavior.
- The mapped `OracleConnectOptions` preserves ordering.
- Unit tests prove config parsing, validation failures, metadata redaction, and final driver option mapping.
- A live test or documented 23ai/FREE script verifies the context through `SYS_CONTEXT(namespace, key)` when the database has an application context available.
close_reason: Implemented typed profile app-context wiring and verified with full Rust gates plus single-bead compliance audit 2026-06-18T08-14-08Z; deterministic missing-test item manually calibrated as false positive against concrete test evidence.

title: Refresh profile docs, setup templates, and doctor for thin-supported fields
description:
## Current gap

The README, setup templates, profile examples, profile metadata, and doctor output still describe a thin profile surface that is behind the current driver capabilities. For example, the README says `os_user` and `program` remain backend-reported unless the driver exposes setters, but the current driver does expose `ClientIdentity` setters. Profile docs also need to reflect that thin edition selection is now driver-supported through `ConnectOptions::with_edition(...)`, while unsupported IAM/external auth paths remain explicit unsupported diagnostics.

## Required change

Refresh user-facing profile/config documentation and diagnostics after the supported thin-driver fields are wired.

## Must cover

- Explicit driver identity fields vs DBMS session attributes.
- Edition selection through the thin driver, including connection-time failure behavior for invalid/unauthorized editions.
- Username/password over TCPS wallet vs unsupported passwordless external/wallet auth.
- Wallet password references and TLS DN/SNI options.
- Proxy auth as supported, distinct from unsupported Kerberos/RADIUS/external auth.
- Application context triples.
- SDU and DRCP routing knobs.
- `[profiles.pool]` behavior after it is either wired or made explicitly non-operative.
- Result materialization improvements for LOB locators and REF CURSORs.

## Security requirements

- `oracle_list_profiles` remains non-secret and does not leak identity values, connect strings, usernames, credential references, wallet paths, wallet passwords, app-context values, proxy target details, or arbitrary edition names unless explicitly justified.
- `oraclemcp --json setup --profile ...` must generate safe templates with placeholders, never real local secret values.
- `doctor` should classify unsupported auth separately from malformed profile config and real connection/auth failures.

## Acceptance criteria

- README profile section is accurate for the thin-only driver.
- `oraclemcp --json setup --profile demo` includes safe examples for the newly wired fields.

title: Materialize generic LOB locator cells with thin driver reads
description:
## Current gap

`rust-oracledb` already exposes public LOB locator reads through `Connection::read_lob` / blocking `read_lob`, and oraclemcp already has capped LOB-reading logic for explicit `oracle_read_clob` style workflows. Generic query serialization still emits placeholders for `QueryValue::Lob` in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs`:

`<unsupported LOB locator size=...>`

That makes ordinary `SELECT clob_col FROM ...` or PL/SQL output returning LOB locators less useful to agents even though the driver can read the locator.

## Required change

Materialize LOB locator cells during generic query/result conversion when the driver returns `QueryValue::Lob`, using the driver's public locator-read API and the existing oraclemcp caps/serialization policy.

## Safety requirements

- Preserve hard caps for text characters and binary bytes.
- Preserve type-specific output: CLOB/NCLOB as capped text, BLOB/BFILE as capped binary/base64 or existing binary representation, with truncation metadata.
- Do not read unbounded LOBs by default.
- Respect call timeout/cancellation handling where available.
- Keep errors structured: unsupported LOB subtype or failed locator read should be explicit, not silently replaced with null.

## Acceptance criteria

- Generic query rows no longer emit unsupported placeholders for supported LOB locators.
- Existing explicit LOB tools keep working.
- Unit tests cover CLOB, BLOB, null LOB, truncation, and locator-read failure paths.
- Live 23ai/FREE tests cover selecting CLOB/BLOB values through `oracle_query` with caps.
- Performance is bounded: tests or benchmarks prove a large LOB cannot force unbounded allocation.
close_reason: Implemented bounded generic LOB locator materialization for CLOB/BLOB/BFILE, wired oracle_query serialization caps through the backend, added unit tests and bound live 23ai coverage; gates, full workspace tests, cargo deny, and single-bead audit passed.

title: Epic: Truthful MCP tool annotations + outputSchema
description:
Idea #4. structuredContent is ALREADY emitted (server.rs:516); the gaps are (a) MCP tool annotations and (b) declared outputSchema. ToolSpec/ToolDescriptor carry only input_schema + an internal `destructive` bool (tools.rs); the wire emits only name/description/inputSchema.
CRITICAL: MCP annotation spec DEFAULTS ARE UNSAFE -- omitted destructiveHint=true, openWorldHint=true, readOnlyHint=false, idempotentHint=false. A fail-closed read-only server MUST set all four explicitly on read tools, or an omitted annotation reads as "destructive, open-world." genai-toolbox actually ships Oracle tools advertised destructive while running read-only; we can be the server that gets this right.
Annotations stay advisory (clients MUST treat them as untrusted) -- the server-side classifier remains the real gate.
Source: res-protocol #2/#3, res-pgpro #10, res-oracle (a)#7.

title: Epic: Read-only DBA diagnostic tool suite
description:
Idea #2/#5/#14 -- the Postgres-MCP-Pro-style differentiator, a perfect fit for a read-only server (pure V$/DBA_* reads + Oracle's own advisor engines).
VERIFIED: oraclemcp-db/src/awr.rs already has top_sql_query + DiagnosticsSource{AwrAsh,Statspack} + detect_statspack + select_diagnostics_source (license-gated, AWR DBA_HIST_* needs Diagnostics Pack, falls back to Statspack STATS$*). NO MCP tool surfaces it yet -- so oracle_top_queries SURFACES existing code, it does not rebuild it.
Health subchecks map to Oracle: invalid objects (DBA_OBJECTS), unusable/unused indexes (DBA_INDEXES,V$OBJECT_USAGE), tablespace+UNDO headroom (DBA_TABLESPACE_USAGE_METRICS), sequence ceiling (DBA_SEQUENCES), disabled/NOVALIDATE constraints (DBA_CONSTRAINTS), buffer cache hit (V$BUFFER_POOL_STATISTICS/V$SYSSTAT). Advisor = DBMS_ADVISOR/DBMS_SQLTUNE (recommendations only, read-compatible; Tuning Pack gated).
Source: res-pgpro #1/#2/#3/#4/#7. All packs must be license-gated like the existing AWR path and preflighted in doctor.
notes: v0.3.0 release decision: defer the read-only DBA diagnostic suite to a post-RC feature wave. title: Epic: Wire OAuth/TLS/host-origin into the binary HTTP transport (S1)
description:
VERIFIED gap (S1): the shipped binary builds HttpTransportConfig::default() at crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:707 -> OAuth None, no TLS/mTLS, JSON-only (no SSE), empty host/origin allowlists, loopback gated behind mandatory --allow-no-auth. Code comment main.rs:753-755 verbatim: "the HTTP transport has no OAuth wired from config yet." The W10 ScopeGrant scope-enforcement is real but reachable only at the dispatch layer / by an embedding caller, NOT through the served HTTP transport -- yet README:121-128 and docs/behavior-inventory.md describe OAuth scope enforcement + TLS as live. This is a truth-in-advertising + security gap.
Operator decision (2026-06-18): WIRE it into the binary (not doc-patch). The auth machinery already exists in oraclemcp-auth (oauth_rs.rs OAuth2.1 RS, scope.rs RBAC, tls.rs rustls); this is config-plumbing, not new crypto.
Children: config plumbing, OAuth validation, TLS/mTLS, session-id validation, protected-resource metadata, e2e tests, doc reconcile.
close_reason: All HTTP auth/TLS child beads are implemented, tested, documented, and closed: config plumbing, OAuth scope enforcement, protected-resource metadata, TLS/mTLS listener, stateful session validation, transport golden/conformance tests, and docs reconciliation.

title: Plumb HttpTransportConfig from CLI/config instead of ::default()
description:
WHY/STATE: main.rs:707 hardcodes HttpTransportConfig::default() (auth off, JSON-only, empty allowlists).
DO: derive HttpTransportConfig from CLI flags + profile/config: OAuth issuer allowlist + audience (RFC8707) + required scopes, host/origin allowlists, JSON-vs-Streamable-SSE mode, stateful session toggle, TLS material paths. Add flags (e.g. --oauth-issuer/--oauth-audience/--require-scope/--allowed-origin/--tls-cert/--tls-key/--http-stateful) with safe fail-closed defaults.
ACCEPTANCE: serve --listen consumes a real config; unit tests cover flag->config mapping; default with no auth flags still refuses routable bind without explicit opt-in.
SOURCE: self-map S1.
close_reason: Implemented binary HTTP transport config from profiles.toml and CLI: host/origin allowlists, JSON/stateful toggles, OAuth resource/issuer/authorization-server/scope/HS256 secret references, fail-closed startup, protected-profile secret policy, and TLS/mTLS config rejection until the native listener is wired. Added unit coverage for flag-to-config mapping and updated README/behavior inventory/robot docs.

title: Wire OAuth 2.1 resource-server validation + scope->level into served HTTP
description:
WHY: W10 ScopeGrant ceilings are tested at the dispatch layer but never supplied by the served HTTP transport.
DO: in serve_http, validate bearer via oraclemcp-auth (oauth_rs.rs: alg=none rejected, issuer allowlist, audience binding, exp/nbf, HS256+injectable RS256/ES256), build ScopeGrant from scopes, carry it into ToolDispatch so apply_oauth_scopes lowers the effective ceiling (min(profile max_level, scope ceiling); protected profiles stay READ_ONLY).
ACCEPTANCE: e2e tests prove narrow token < broad token < profile ceiling, protected profile pinned READ_ONLY, missing/invalid token -> 401 + WWW-Authenticate, insufficient scope -> 403. All through the binary HTTP server, not just DispatchContext.
SOURCE: self-map S1, res-protocol #10.
close_reason: Implemented OAuth 2.1 bearer enforcement through served HTTP: valid tokens carry scopes into ToolDispatch, scope ceilings are applied end-to-end, missing/invalid tokens return 401 WWW-Authenticate, insufficient scope returns 403, metadata remains public, and narrow/broad/profile ceilings are covered by binary HTTP e2e tests. Verified: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace; cargo deny check; scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh; scripts/sensitive_data_lint.sh.

title: Wire rustls TLS/mTLS into the binary HTTP listener
description:
WHY: oraclemcp-auth/src/tls.rs build_server_config (rustls, optional client-cert verify) is never invoked by the binary.
DO: when TLS material is configured, wrap the TcpListener with rustls; support optional mTLS client-cert verification; keep plaintext path behind --allow-no-auth only.
ACCEPTANCE: TLS handshake test; mTLS required-client-cert test; plaintext still requires explicit opt-in.
SOURCE: self-map S1.
close_reason: Wired native rustls TLS/mTLS into the binary HTTP listener. Added serve_https/serve_https_until using the existing HTTP parser after TLS termination, proper close_notify teardown, CLI/config PEM loading, mTLS as an auth mode, accurate HTTP/HTTPS startup warnings, docs updates, server-only TLS handshake test, mTLS rejects-no-client-cert/accepts-client-cert test, and binary PEM-path config test. Verified: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p oraclemcp-core http::tests::serve_https -- --nocapture; cargo test -p oraclemcp http_tls_material_builds_native_tls_config -- --nocapture; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace; cargo deny check; scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh; scripts/sensitive_data_lint.sh.

title: Validate mcp-session-id on subsequent stateful HTTP requests
description:
WHY/STATE: self-map: mcp-session-id is emitted on stateful init but NEVER validated on later requests.
DO: persist issued session ids; reject/relabel requests bearing unknown/expired session ids per Streamable HTTP semantics; keep stateless mode working.
ACCEPTANCE: test that a forged/stale session id is rejected; valid session id round-trips.
SOURCE: self-map protocol gaps.
close_reason: Implemented stateful Streamable HTTP mcp-session-id validation. Initialize registers issued ids, subsequent stateful POST/DELETE requests require a known id, forged/missing ids are rejected before dispatch, DELETE invalidates ids so stale ids fail, and listener wrappers install a session store automatically. Updated golden harness and behavior inventory. Verified: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test -p oraclemcp-core http::tests::stateful -- --nocapture; cargo test -p oraclemcp-core --test golden_behavior golden_http_stateful_streamable_session -- --nocapture; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test --workspace; cargo deny check; scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh; scripts/sensitive_data_lint.sh.

title: Serve protected-resource metadata + WWW-Authenticate from the binary
description:
WHY: RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata route and WWW-Authenticate are part of the OAuth RS contract but only reachable via non-default config.

ACCEPTANCE: metadata document validates against RFC 9728; 401 carries correct WWW-Authenticate.
SOURCE: res-protocol #10, self-map.
close_reason: Verified and tightened binary HTTP OAuth metadata/challenge contract: /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource is public and returns resource, authorization_servers, bearer_methods_supported, and scopes_supported; 401 challenges point at the metadata URL exactly; invalid tokens include invalid_token; insufficient scope returns 403 with insufficient_scope. Verified through crates/oraclemcp/tests/e2e_http_oauth.rs plus cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, cargo deny check, scripts/oraclemcp_boundary_lint.sh, scripts/sensitive_data_lint.sh.

Convert the research's converged positioning into documented, test-backed guarantees: "the safe-by-construction, precision-correct, single-binary read-only Oracle MCP." Neither SQLcl (privilege-delegated, write-capable, JVM) nor genai-toolbox (Query-vs-Exec, no parser, flag-flippable, NUMBER->lossy float) statically guarantees no mutation, and genai-toolbox loses NUMBER precision (int64/float64).

title: Epic: Serve and advertise the built-but-unserved MCP protocol surface
description:
VERIFIED gap (S2): crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:193-195 advertises only {"capabilities":{"tools":{}}}; the dispatcher (server.rs:369-373) handles only initialize / notifications/initialized / tools/list / tools/call. But resources.rs (resource templates like oracle://object/{owner}/{type}/{name} + a 5-playbook prompt catalog) and subscriptions.rs (resources/subscribe + resources/updated) are fully built, exported, and tested -- just never served. Spec-compliant clients gate resources/list, prompts/list, resources/subscribe, completion/complete on capability negotiation, so this shipped code is DEAD to strict clients.
Idea #1 (highest ROI, additive, no new safety surface): advertise + wire the dispatcher arms.
Source: res-protocol #1, self-map S2. Spec revision in tree: protocolVersion 2025-11-25 (server.rs:193).
notes: v0.3.0 release decision: defer the remaining unserved subscription/update surface. The stable RC serves and advertises resources/list, resources/read, resources/templates/list, prompts/list, and prompts/get with conformance coverage; resources/subscribe remains intentionally unadvertised until durable DBMS_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION or polling semantics are designed and tested.

title: Advertise resources/prompts/completions/listChanged in initialize

WHY/STATE: server.rs:194 emits only {"tools":{}} so built resources/prompts/subscriptions are invisible to compliant clients.
DO: emit capabilities reflecting what is actually served: tools{listChanged}, resources{subscribe,listChanged}, prompts{listChanged}, completions{} -- only flip each on as its dispatcher arm lands (do not advertise unserved features).
ACCEPTANCE: initialize result advertises served capabilities; conformance asserts negotiation; nothing advertised that 404s.
SOURCE: res-protocol #1, self-map S2. Additive, no new safety surface.
close_reason: Advertised only served MCP capabilities: tools/listChanged=false; added conformance coverage proving resources, prompts, and completion remain unadvertised until dispatcher arms exist; refreshed golden transcripts.

title: Wire resources/list + resources/read + resources/templates/list arms
description:
WHY: resources.rs (16KB, templates incl. oracle://object/{owner}/{type}/{name}) is built but not dispatched.
DO: add server.rs dispatcher arms for resources/list, resources/read, resources/templates/list backed by resources.rs; enforce that resource reads honor the active session lease + operating level + per-DB ceiling (only expose what the current grant can see).
ACCEPTANCE: resources resolve; authz parity with reads; golden+conformance tests.
SAFETY: resource reads route through the same fail-closed/authz path as oracle_query.
SOURCE: res-protocol #1, res-universal (schema-as-resources).
close_reason: Wired served MCP resource methods: initialize advertises resources with subscribe/listChanged false; resources/list and resources/templates/list respond; resources/read serves capabilities/tools directly and schema/object resources through the existing guarded tool dispatch path; prompts/completion/session resources remain unadvertised. Covered by conformance, golden fixtures, full workspace tests, and release gates.

title: Wire prompts/list + prompts/get dispatcher arms
description:

ACCEPTANCE: prompts resolve; conformance tests; advertised only after this lands.
SOURCE: res-protocol #1.
close_reason: Served the built-in prompt catalog over prompts/list and prompts/get, advertised prompts only after handlers existed, validated prompt arguments with structured JSON-RPC error data, and kept completion unadvertised. title: Wire resources/subscribe + resources/updated notifications
description:
WHY: subscriptions.rs (resources/subscribe + resources/updated registry) is built but not served.

ACCEPTANCE: subscribe -> receive updated on change; capability resources{subscribe:true} only after this lands.

notes: v0.3.0 release decision: defer. resources/subscribe needs durable event semantics and Oracle DBMS_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION or documented polling behavior. Shipping this late would expand the protocol surface and risk unstable promises; current initialize truthfully advertises resources.subscribe=false.

Assert unserved arms such as completion and subscriptions remain unadvertised until implemented; subscription behavior is tracked separately in oraclemcp-epic-proto-surface-57t.4.

title: Decision: keep oracle_session (guarded-write router) UNWIRED in the read-only binary
description:
WHY/STATE: session_tool.rs:194-336 implements a full lease/escalation/transaction router with step-up, but it is deliberately NOT in TOOL_NAMES (verified absent from registry.rs). AGENTS.md:46-48: the guarded-write machinery is built but deliberately not surfaced by THIS binary (writes are the sibling plsql-mcp's job).
DO: record this as an explicit, durable decision (code comment + behavior-inventory line) so a future agent does not "fix" the unserved tool by surfacing writes. If writes are ever desired they belong behind a separate, opt-in build, not the read-only release.
ACCEPTANCE: decision documented next to session_tool.rs and in behavior-inventory.
close_reason: Documented the durable decision next to session_tool.rs and in behavior-inventory: oracle_session remains unadvertised in the read-only oraclemcp binary unless a future explicit opt-in surface adds separate gates. title: Epic: Read-only defense-in-depth + auditability
description:
Ideas #3 + #6. Double down on the core identity (safe-by-construction read-only).
(1) Session-level read-only backstop so the guarantee is two-layer, not classifier-only -- even a hypothetical classifier bug cannot mutate. (2) SQLcl-style auditability: per-statement marker comment + dynamic V$SESSION tagging so a DBA can see/trust MCP traffic WITHOUT trusting the client. (3) prompt-injection output fencing for returned rows.
VERIFIED: connection.rs:504-537 already applies DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO.SET_MODULE/SET_ACTION/SET_CLIENT_INFO + client_identifier from profile session_identity (static) -- so dynamic LLM/agent tagging EXTENDS existing plumbing, not net-new.
Source: res-universal #2/#8, res-oracle (a)#1/#2 (DBTOOLS$MCP_LOG, /* LLM in use */ marker, MODULE/ACTION).
notes: v0.3.0 release decision: defer as a post-RC defense-in-depth wave. The RC already preserves the fail-closed SQL guard and profile/session gates; adding transaction backstops, audit-marker comments, dynamic V tags, and output fencing changes DB session/response semantics and needs a dedicated live regression cycle instead of late-release churn.

title: Wire explicit thin-driver client identity fields from profiles
description:
## Current gap

`rust-oracledb` already accepts a full `ClientIdentity` with `program`, `machine`, `osuser`, `terminal`, and `driver_name`. oraclemcp only exposes `session_identity.module`, `client_identifier`, and `driver_name`, then currently maps `module -> program`, process `$HOSTNAME -> machine`, process `$USER -> osuser`, and `client_identifier -> terminal` in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs`.

That means operators cannot deliberately set the Oracle-visible client identity fields that the thin driver already supports. It also makes the README stale because it says the driver lacks setters, while the driver now has them.

## Required change

Extend profile/session identity config and DB connect options so operators can set driver-level identity fields explicitly:

- `session_identity.program`
- `session_identity.machine`
- `session_identity.os_user` or `osuser` with one documented spelling
- `session_identity.terminal`

Preserve existing `module`, `action`, `client_identifier`, and `client_info` behavior as Oracle session attributes set after connect via `DBMS_APPLICATION_INFO` / `DBMS_SESSION`.

## Implementation notes

- Keep backwards compatibility: if the new driver-level fields are absent, retain current defaults/mapping so existing profiles still work.
- Prefer explicit driver fields over overloaded values when present.
- Keep `driver_name` in the connect-time identity.
- Do not expose configured identity values in `oracle_list_profiles`; these can identify operators, hosts, tenants, or workflows.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config schema accepts and inherits the new identity fields.
- `profile_to_options` carries them into `OracleConnectOptions`.
- `RustOracleConnection` maps them into `oracledb::protocol::ClientIdentity`.
- Unit tests prove defaults, explicit overrides, inheritance, and metadata redaction.
- README/setup templates accurately distinguish driver-level identity from DBMS session attributes.
- A live 23ai/FREE verification note or test checks `SYS_CONTEXT`/`V$SESSION` fields where privileges allow it.
close_reason: Implemented explicit thin driver identity fields with profile/core/db mapping, redaction, README/setup updates, unit tests, workspace gates, and gated live-test path

title: Add live thin-driver profile/config integration matrix
description:
## Current gap

The individual wiring tasks can be unit-tested, but the risk is in the full profile-to-driver path: TOML config -> inheritance -> secret resolution -> `SessionContext` -> `OracleConnectOptions` -> `oracledb::ConnectOptions` -> live Oracle session diagnostics. There is not yet a focused live thin-driver profile matrix that proves the supported fields work together on Oracle 23ai/FREE.

## Required change

Add a focused integration-test matrix for the profile/config capabilities that are wireable now.

## Matrix should cover

- username/password basic thin connection
- username/password over TCPS wallet when a wallet fixture/environment is available
- explicit client identity fields: program, machine, os user, terminal, driver name
- DBMS session attributes: module, action, client identifier, client info
- edition selection verified through `SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','CURRENT_EDITION_NAME')`, including invalid/unauthorized edition failure behavior
- proxy auth where live grants are available
- SDU override and DRCP connect-string transformation without leaking connect strings
- application context triples where a test context is available
- generic LOB locator materialization with caps
- REF CURSOR / implicit result set serialization with caps and cleanup

## Test design

- Unit tests must run without Oracle.
- Live tests must be gated by explicit environment variables and skip cleanly when unavailable.
- Live logs must redact usernames, passwords, wallet paths, wallet passwords, connect strings, tokens, identity metadata, proxy details, and arbitrary edition names unless explicitly asserted as part of live session diagnostics.
- The matrix should be runnable locally against Oracle 23ai/FREE and in CI when secrets are present.

## Acceptance criteria

- A documented command runs the local non-secret unit/profile test subset.
- A documented command runs the live 23ai/FREE profile matrix when env vars are present.
- Skipped live tests explain exactly which env var or database prerequisite is missing.
- At least one test verifies `oracle_connection_info` sees configured identity/session fields where privileges allow.
- The test suite protects against regressions where newly wired profile fields parse but never reach the driver.
close_reason: Added the live thin-driver profile/config matrix, documented unit/live commands, ran the profile subset and full live_oracle against Oracle 23ai, ran full workspace gates and cargo deny, and verified with single-bead compliance audits 2026-06-18T10-23-20Z/10-24-24Z plus closer evidence.

title: Wire thin TCPS wallet password and TLS options from profiles
description:
## Current gap

The current `rust-oracledb` driver exposes TCPS/wallet and TLS controls that oraclemcp does not fully surface:

- `ConnectOptions::with_wallet_password(...)`
- `with_ssl_server_dn_match(...)`
- `with_ssl_server_cert_dn(...)`
- `with_use_sni(...)`
- `with_wallet_location(...)`

oraclemcp currently only passes `oci.wallet_location` and then unconditionally sets `use_sni(true)` when a wallet location exists. There is no profile-safe wallet password reference, no explicit server DN controls, and no way for an operator to choose SNI behavior except by embedding some options in the connect string.

## Required change

Add explicit profile config for currently supported TCPS/wallet options, then map them into `OracleConnectOptions` and `oracledb::ConnectOptions`:

- wallet location, preserving existing `[profiles.oci].wallet_location`
- wallet password through a secret reference, not a literal TOML value
- `ssl_server_dn_match`
- `ssl_server_cert_dn`
- `use_sni`

## Security requirements

- Wallet passwords must be resolved through the same safe credential path style as `credential_ref` or an equally explicit `wallet_password_ref` field.
- Wallet password values, wallet paths, connect strings, usernames, and DN values that could reveal topology must not leak through `Debug`, doctor output, profile metadata, or error envelopes.
- Redaction tests must include driver errors containing wallet path + wallet password + connect string.

## Acceptance criteria

- Strict config parsing accepts the new fields and rejects misspellings.
- Inheritance works for the new TCPS fields.
- `profile_to_options` carries all fields to `OracleConnectOptions`.
- `to_connect_options` applies the values with the driver's `with_*` methods.
- Existing wallet profiles keep working with the previous default behavior unless a new explicit value overrides it.
- Unit tests cover mapping, inheritance, default behavior, and redaction.
- README/setup templates explain when to use `connect_string` query parameters vs named profile fields.
close_reason: Implemented thin TCPS wallet password and TLS profile wiring with safe wallet_password_ref resolution, driver builder mapping, redaction hardening, docs, setup templates, unit tests, workspace gates, and gated live-test hooks

title: Wire supported thin proxy authentication from profiles
description:
## Current gap

oraclemcp already has `AuthAdapter::Proxy { proxy_user, target_schema }` in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/auth_adapter.rs`, and the current thin driver exposes `ConnectOptions::with_proxy_user(...)`. The profile schema and `profile_to_options` path do not expose or apply this capability, so proxy authentication remains stranded code.

## Required change

Add a strict profile configuration shape for supported proxy authentication and wire it into the thin connect path. The profile should make the two identities unambiguous:

- the authenticating proxy account that owns the password/credential reference
- the target schema/client identity that Oracle should proxy into

Then map that to the driver's `with_proxy_user(...)` in the form the driver expects, using the existing `AuthAdapter::Proxy` validation helpers where appropriate.

## Explicit non-scope

Do not implement Kerberos, RADIUS/native MFA, OCI IAM database-token auth, or passwordless external/wallet auth in this bead. Those are not supported by the current driver surface and should continue to fail explicitly.

## Security and safety requirements

- Proxy config must not weaken profile `max_level`, `default_level`, `protected`, or signed-tool requirements.
- Proxy account names and target schemas should not leak through `oracle_list_profiles` unless there is an intentional, documented non-secret metadata field.
- Errors must distinguish incomplete proxy config from unsupported enterprise auth.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config accepts a documented proxy auth shape and rejects incomplete proxy settings.
- `profile_to_options` carries proxy intent into `OracleConnectOptions`.
- `to_connect_options` calls the driver's `with_proxy_user(...)` correctly.
- Unit tests cover valid proxy config, missing proxy user, missing target schema, inheritance, redaction, and preservation of profile operating-level gates.
- A live test plan documents the required Oracle grants, for example `ALTER USER target GRANT CONNECT THROUGH proxy`, and verifies session identity where privileges allow.
close_reason: Implemented strict thin proxy_auth profile shape, inheritance/validation, AuthAdapter and OracleConnectOptions wiring, driver with_proxy_user mapping, redaction hardening, docs/setup/live-test hooks, and workspace gates; live smoke currently skips because configured DB credentials return ORA-01017

title: Epic: native Asupersync runtime adoption
description: Tracks replacement of Tokio runtime assumptions with Asupersync context, scopes, cancellation, budgets, admission, shutdown, deterministic tests, and runtime-native primitives. Asupersync is not an executor swap: controlled APIs must take &Cx, spawned work must be region-owned, and tests must prove cancellation/quiescence behavior.
design: This epic owns the runtime semantic migration. Asupersync is adopted for structured concurrency, Cx propagation, cancellation, budgets, region-owned tasks, deterministic testing, and capability narrowing. Do not treat it as an executor swap. Controlled async APIs should accept &Cx, long loops should checkpoint, spawned work should belong to a request/tool region, and cancellation must leave resources clean.
acceptance_criteria: Tool dispatch and DB paths propagate &Cx; direct Tokio imports are gone from production code; shutdown, timeout, admission, cancellation cleanup, panic safety, and deterministic LabRuntime tests pass without ambient Tokio runtime handles.
notes: Child scope: W2 nightly pin, W6a pure dispatch, W6b DB dispatch, W7a runtime primitives, W7b DB cancellation cleanup, and W11 deterministic tests. Closure requires no production Tokio runtime dependency, no ambient runtime-handle assumptions, and tests for shutdown, admission, timeout, cancellation cleanup, quiescence, and panic/redaction safety.
close_reason: All runtime migration children are closed: nightly CI, Cx propagation through pure and DB dispatch, Asupersync runtime primitives, DB cancellation cleanup, and deterministic Asupersync tests. Production Tokio/runtime-handle dependencies are absent; shutdown, timeout, admission, cancellation cleanup, quiescence, preview-token races, and redaction behavior are covered by local and CI gates.

title: Epic: native MCP transports
description: Tracks removing rmcp, Axum, Hyper, and SDK transport assumptions from oraclemcp while preserving MCP behavior for stdio and HTTP clients. Native stdio proves the MCP core first; MCP conformance tests gate the more complex Streamable HTTP implementation.

Native stdio is deliberately first because it proves the MCP core before HTTP session complexity. HTTP must preserve Streamable HTTP and protected-resource metadata; dropping HTTP behavior is not an acceptable simplification.

title: Expose thin SDU and DRCP routing settings in profiles
description:
## Current gap

The current thin driver supports an explicit `ConnectOptions::with_sdu(...)`, and the local oraclemcp DB crate already has `DrcpConfig` for appending `server=pooled`, `pool_connection_class`, and `pool_purity` to EZConnect strings. The strict profile schema does not expose either as first-class profile config, so operators must hand-edit connect strings or cannot use the local `DrcpConfig` at all.

## Required change

Expose and wire supported thin network/session routing knobs from profiles:

- SDU request size mapped to `oracledb::ConnectOptions::with_sdu(...)`
- DRCP pooled-server settings using existing `oraclemcp_db::DrcpConfig`
- documented interaction when a connect string already includes query parameters such as `wallet_location`

## Design requirements

- Keep defaults unchanged: no DRCP unless explicitly configured, driver default SDU unless explicitly configured.
- Validate SDU ranges at config load or map through the driver's documented clamp with tests that make the behavior explicit.
- Do not log full connect strings after DRCP parameters are appended.
- Keep this separate from `[profiles.pool]`; DRCP is an Oracle server-routing feature, while the local pool is an oraclemcp client-side reuse feature.

## Acceptance criteria

- Config schema accepts documented DRCP and SDU fields.
- Inheritance works for DRCP and SDU fields.
- `profile_to_options` applies DRCP before building `OracleConnectOptions.connect_string`.
- `to_connect_options` applies SDU through the driver builder.
- Unit tests cover dedicated server default, DRCP with class/purity, existing connect-string query params, SDU default/override, and redaction.
- README profile examples explain when to prefer named fields over raw connect-string query parameters.
close_reason: Implemented typed profile SDU and DRCP routing fields with validation, inheritance, profile-to-driver mapping, redaction-safe diagnostics/docs, setup templates, and tests. Verified with git diff --check, cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, cargo deny check, plus calibrated single-bead compliance audit note.

title: Wire thin edition selection from profiles
description:
## Problem

oraclemcp already exposes `session_identity.edition` in profile config and carries it through `oraclemcp_core::profile_to_options`, but `oraclemcp_db::connection::apply_session_identity` still rejects any edition value with `UnsupportedFeature("edition-based redefinition selection is not supported by the published thin driver yet")`. The current pure-Rust thin driver now exposes `ConnectOptions::with_edition(...)` and applies it during authentication via `AUTH_ORA_EDITION`, so this is now an oraclemcp wiring gap rather than an upstream blocker.

## Required implementation

- Preserve the existing profile field name and inheritance behavior for `session_identity.edition`.
- Add `edition` to the `OracleConnectOptions` -> thin `ConnectOptions` bridge and call the driver edition option before connect/authentication.
- Remove the stale unsupported-feature rejection for edition from `apply_session_identity`.
- Keep edition values secret-safe in diagnostics and logs; do not echo arbitrary profile values in doctor output unless the tool is explicitly returning live Oracle session diagnostics.
- Preserve fail-closed SQL guard behavior and read-only operating-level ceilings.

## Acceptance criteria

- Unit tests prove profile edition config is mapped into `OracleConnectOptions` and then into the thin driver options.
- A regression test proves setting `session_identity.edition` no longer returns the stale unsupported-feature diagnostic before connect.
- Live Oracle 23ai/FREE validation instructions include checking `SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','CURRENT_EDITION_NAME')` after connecting with a valid edition and verifying invalid/unauthorized editions fail at connect time with a structured server error.
- Docs, setup templates, and doctor/help text no longer describe thin edition selection as unsupported once this lands.

## Out of scope

- Creating or managing Oracle editions.
- Falling back silently to the database default edition when an explicit edition fails.
- Supporting non-thin/native thick mode.
notes: Unblocked: crates.io now has oracledb 0.2.1 (cargo search verified 2026-06-18), and local rust-oracledb 0.2.1 exposes ConnectOptions::with_edition. Proceed by updating oraclemcp from oracledb 0.1.1 to 0.2.1 and wiring session_identity.edition.
close_reason: Wired released thin edition selection via oracledb 0.2.1, added tests/docs/live validation, and recorded audit defense for deterministic false positive.

title: Serialize REF CURSOR and implicit result sets from thin results
description:
## Current gap

The current thin driver exposes enough public cursor APIs to consume REF CURSOR values now: `QueryValue::Cursor` includes a cursor id and column metadata, and blocking `fetch_rows` / `fetch_rows_with_columns` / `release_cursor` APIs exist. oraclemcp currently serializes generic `QueryValue::Cursor` as an unsupported placeholder in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs`.

Agents therefore cannot inspect stored procedure outputs or PL/SQL blocks that return REF CURSORs, even when the driver has already received the cursor handle.

## Required change

Expand oraclemcp's thin result conversion so supported REF CURSOR values and implicit result sets are converted into bounded nested result objects.

## Safety requirements

- Enforce row, cell, byte, and nesting caps.
- Preserve child cursor column metadata.
- Release/close cursor ids deterministically, including error paths.
- Do not let child cursor fetching bypass the SQL guard; only consume cursors returned by already-authorized statements/procedures.
- Mark truncation explicitly when caps stop fetching.

## Acceptance criteria

- REF CURSOR cells no longer render as unsupported placeholders when the driver supplies a valid cursor id.
- Returned JSON includes child result columns, rows, row_count/fetched_count, and truncation metadata.
- Cursor release is tested on success and failure.
- Implicit result sets are surfaced if the driver's `QueryResult.implicit_resultsets` contains cursor values; unsupported shapes remain explicit.
- Unit tests cover zero-row cursor, multi-row cursor, caps, failure cleanup, and nested result serialization.
- A live 23ai/FREE test uses a small PL/SQL block or package procedure returning a REF CURSOR.
close_reason: Implemented bounded REF CURSOR and implicit-resultset serialization for thin results, including nested result JSON, row/cell/byte/depth caps, cursor expression and DBMS_SQL.RETURN_RESULT live 23ai coverage, failure-path cleanup coverage, full workspace gates, cargo deny, live feature tests, and single-bead compliance audit with closer evidence.

title: Program: thin-native Asupersync oraclemcp migration
description: Program epic for replacing oraclemcp's thick ODPI-C Oracle backend, Tokio runtime, rmcp SDK transport, Axum, and Hyper with a pure-Rust thin Oracle driver, native Asupersync runtime semantics, and native MCP transports. The safety invariant is non-negotiable: raw SQL must remain fail-closed through oraclemcp-guard before any database execution, async gap, connection acquisition, or mutable execution state. This epic is complete only when the dependency graph below is implemented, tested, release-gated, and documented.
design: User value: oraclemcp becomes a smaller, easier-to-install, pure-Rust Oracle MCP server with no Instant Client dependency, while preserving the fail-closed read-only SQL safety model. Architecture decision: this is a hard migration, not a compat layer. Final production dependencies must not include Tokio, rmcp, axum, hyper, hyper-util, oracle, odpic-sys, r2d2, or asupersync-tokio-compat. The migration is complete only after tests, dependency gates, docs, release artifacts, and registry automation all agree on the thin-native architecture.
acceptance_criteria: All child migration beads are closed; production builds are thin-only and native Asupersync; forbidden dependency gate passes; release artifacts and docs describe thin-native behavior; no credential, PII, or sensitive business data leaks through logs, fixtures, errors, panic paths, or generated artifacts.
notes: Global invariants for all child beads: never introduce unsafe; never weaken oraclemcp-guard; classify raw SQL before await/connection/lease/mutable execution state; keep session leases, rollback semantics, NUMBER/string output, NLS-stable serialization, AWR/ASH license gating, and preview-token single-use behavior; no secrets, PII, or sensitive business data in logs, fixtures, doctor, errors, panic paths, or generated artifacts.
close_reason: All child migration beads and epics are closed. Production builds are thin-only on published oracledb and native Asupersync, with no Tokio/rmcp/Axum/Hyper/oracle/ODPI-C/r2d2 production dependencies. Guard-before-I/O, session cleanup, native MCP stdio/HTTP, OAuth scope enforcement, release artifacts, CI dependency gates, performance evidence, Docker smoke, MCP registry smoke, and sensitive-data checks are implemented and verified locally plus CI run 27570414583 on 7f70f58.
